# What Impact Have Corporate Giving Programs Had on Women's Access to Tech Education and Careers?

Corporate initiatives have significantly advanced women's tech education and careers, fostering diversity, mentorship, professional networking, and innovation. Programs offer scholarships, internships, and encourage female-led startups, besides promoting a culture of inclusion and equality. Access to the latest tech and training, increased female visibility in the industry, and the creation of more diverse teams highlight the long-term positive impact on tech innovation.

Corporate initiatives have significantly advanced women's tech education and careers, fostering diversity, mentorship, professional networking, and innovation. Programs offer scholarships, internships, and encourage female-led startups, besides promoting a culture of inclusion and equality. Access to the latest tech and training, increased female visibility in the industry, and the creation of more diverse teams highlight the long-term positive impact on tech innovation.

![](https://www.womentech.net/sites/default/files/votingapi_reaction/unhelpgul-whitebg.svg)
![](https://www.womentech.net/sites/default/files/votingapi_reaction/helpful-whitebg.svg)
![]()
![](/sites/default/files/default_images/Women%20in%20Tech%20profile%20picture.png)

### Bridging the Gender Gap in Tech

Corporate giving programs have significantly contributed to increasing women's access to tech education and careers, helping bridge the longstanding gender gap in the industry. By providing scholarships, internships, and mentorship programs targeted at women, these initiatives have opened doors for many to pursue studies and careers in technology, areas traditionally dominated by men.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Enhancing Diversity in the Tech Workplace

The involvement of corporations in supporting women's access to tech education and careers has been a catalyst for enhancing diversity within the tech industry. By investing in programs that encourage the participation of women, companies not only contribute to a more inclusive workforce but also benefit from the diverse perspectives and innovative ideas that women bring to the table.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Mentorship Opportunities

Corporate giving programs have played a pivotal role in providing mentorship opportunities for women aspiring to enter the tech field. Mentorship has proven to be invaluable, offering guidance, motivation, and real-world insights that textbooks cannot provide. These relationships foster a supportive network that empowers women to navigate their careers in tech successfully.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Creation of Professional Networks

One of the impactful outcomes of corporate giving initiatives is the creation and expansion of professional networks for women in tech. Through conferences, workshops, and meetups sponsored by corporations, women have the opportunity to connect with peers and industry leaders, enhancing their career prospects and professional development.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Encouragement of Innovation and Entrepreneurship

Corporate programs aimed at supporting women in tech often include funding and resources for startups led by women. This encouragement of innovation and entrepreneurship not only helps in the creation of new tech ventures but also promotes a culture where women feel empowered to lead and innovate, challenging the status quo.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Scholarship Programs for Women

A direct impact of corporate giving has been the increase in scholarship programs specifically designed for women pursuing education in technology. These scholarships alleviate the financial burden and open educational opportunities that might otherwise be inaccessible, enabling more women to prepare for careers in the dynamic tech sector.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Fostering a Culture of Inclusion and Equality

Beyond financial support and mentorship, corporate giving programs have contributed to fostering a culture of inclusion and equality within the tech industry. They raise awareness about the importance of gender diversity, influencing policies and practices within corporations to create a more welcoming environment for women.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Access to Cutting-Edge Technology and Training

Corporate giving programs provide women with access to the latest technology and advanced training opportunities. From coding boot camps to state-of-the-art software, gaining hands-on experience with cutting-edge tools prepares women for competitive roles in the tech field, ensuring they are not left behind in a rapidly evolving industry.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Increased Visibility and Representation

The success of corporate giving programs in promoting women's access to tech education and careers has increased the visibility and representation of women in the industry. Role models and success stories of women in tech serve as inspiration for younger generations, making the tech field more attractive and accessible to them.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Long-Term Impact on Industry Innovation

Lastly, the inclusion of more women in tech through corporate giving programs is not just a win for gender equality but also for industry innovation. Diverse teams have been shown to be more creative and better at problem-solving, indicating that these programs are contributing to the broader advancement of technology and society.

![]()
![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

This section is for sharing any additional examples, stories, or insights that do not fit into previous sections. Is there anything else you'd like to add?

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

# How Are Grassroots Tech Access Programs Transforming Opportunities for Women in Technology?

Grassroots tech access programs are crucial for empowering women and ensuring gender diversity in the tech sector. They provide practical skills training, access to technology, supportive networks, and mentorship, helping to overcome economic and psychological barriers. These initiatives are instrumental in increasing women's participation in technology, fostering innovation, and addressing skills shortages. Their success also encourages policy changes, promoting broader gender equality and economic growth.

Grassroots tech access programs are crucial for empowering women and ensuring gender diversity in the tech sector. They provide practical skills training, access to technology, supportive networks, and mentorship, helping to overcome economic and psychological barriers. These initiatives are instrumental in increasing women's participation in technology, fostering innovation, and addressing skills shortages. Their success also encourages policy changes, promoting broader gender equality and economic growth.

![](https://www.womentech.net/sites/default/files/votingapi_reaction/unhelpgul-whitebg.svg)
![](https://www.womentech.net/sites/default/files/votingapi_reaction/helpful-whitebg.svg)
![](/sites/default/files/default_images/Women%20in%20Tech%20profile%20picture.png)

### Empowering Through Education

Grassroots tech access programs are playing a pivotal role in breaking down the gender barriers within the technology sector by providing tailored educational workshops and courses for women. These initiatives offer practical skills in coding, app development, and cybersecurity, among others, helping women gain the confidence and knowledge needed to pursue careers in technology. This educational empowerment is a key driver in increasing female participation and diversification in the tech workforce.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Increasing Access to Technology for Women

Grassroots tech access programs are significantly transforming opportunities for women by making technology more accessible. By offering resources such as free or low-cost computer access, these programs help overcome economic barriers that disproportionately affect women, particularly in underserved communities. This increased access not only facilitates women's entry into tech-related fields but also inspires them to become innovators and leaders in technology.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Creating Inclusive Networks

One of the transformative aspects of grassroots tech access programs for women in technology is the creation of supportive and inclusive networks. These platforms provide women with mentorship opportunities, peer support, and professional connections, helping them navigate the challenges of the tech industry. Such networks are crucial for sharing knowledge, opening up career opportunities, and building confidence among women tech enthusiasts and professionals.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Promoting Gender Diversity in Tech

Grassroots tech access programs are crucial in promoting gender diversity within the technology sector. By specifically targeting women, these programs help to balance the gender scales in an industry historically dominated by men. This not only benefits women by opening up new career paths but also enriches the tech community with diverse perspectives, leading to more innovative solutions and products.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Bridging the Technology Gender Gap in Rural Areas

In rural and remote areas, grassroots tech access programs are vital in bridging the gender gap in technology. These initiatives provide much-needed resources and training to women who may otherwise be left behind in the digital age. By reaching out to these communities, the programs ensure that women in rural areas have the opportunity to develop tech skills, ultimately contributing to their economic empowerment and the eradication of digital gender divides.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Boosting Economic Opportunities for Women

Grassroots tech access programs are not only about providing women with the skills to enter the tech industry but also about boosting their economic opportunities. By equipping women with in-demand tech skills, these programs enhance their employability, opening up higher-paying job opportunities. This has a ripple effect, contributing to economic growth and reducing gender income disparities.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Building Confidence and Reducing Impostor Syndrome

Grassroots tech access programs also play a psychological role by building confidence among women and reducing impostor syndrome in tech environments. Through supportive learning communities and the success stories of female role models, these programs help women overcome self-doubt and feel more entitled to their place in technology. This is a fundamental step towards achieving gender parity in tech roles.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Fostering Innovation and Creativity

By involving more women in technology through grassroots access programs, the industry benefits from increased innovation and creativity. Diverse teams have been shown to produce more innovative results, and by empowering women to bring their unique perspectives and ideas to the table, these programs contribute to a more competitive and dynamic technology sector.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Addressing Skills Shortages

Grassroots tech access programs for women are also a response to the global skills shortages in the technology sector. By training women to fill these gaps, not only are the individual prospects of women improved, but the technology industry as a whole becomes more robust and resilient. These programs ensure a steady influx of talent, ready to tackle the challenges of tomorrow.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### Catalyzing Policy Change

Lastly, the success of grassroots tech access programs for women has the potential to influence policy decisions. By demonstrating the positive impacts of such programs on communities and economies, policymakers are encouraged to support and fund similar initiatives. This can lead to broader changes in education, workforce development, and gender equality policies, further transforming opportunities for women in technology.

![](/sites/default/files/styles/thumbnail/public/default_images/shutterstock_1186973788.jpg)

### What else to take into account

This section is for sharing any additional examples, stories, or insights that do not fit into previous sections. Is there anything else you'd like to add?

--- SOURCE 5: Technology as a Driver of Gender Equality and Peace - CSIS ---
URL: https://www.csis.org/analysis/technology-driver-gender-equality-and-peace

SUMMARY:
Moreover, training and paying women to report issues, monitor progress, and collect data on SPCPS progress across their communities can serve as an opportunity to connect and collaborate with local women, building on the work of organizations that already have existing programming using technology to combat gender inequality and fragility. *Abigail Edwards is a research assistant with the Project on Fragility and Mobility at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) in Washington, D.C. Alexis Day is program manager for the Smart Women, Smart Power Initiative and outreach manager for the International Security Program at CSIS.

FULL CONTENT:
[Skip to main content](#main-content)

# Technology as a Driver of Gender Equality and Peace

Commentary
by 

Abigail Edwards, 

[Alexis Day](/people/alexis-day), 
and 
[Erol Yayboke](/people/erol-yayboke)

Published March 8, 2023

Between March 6–17, UN member states, UN agencies, and NGOs from around the world will meet at the [67th Commission on the Status of Women](https://www.unwomen.org/en/csw/csw67-2023) (CSW). Established in 1946, the [CSW](https://www.unwomen.org/en/csw) has been the primary forum through which the United Nations—and the broader international system—has promoted gender equality and the empowerment of women. This year’s theme is innovation, technological change, and education in the digital age, with an overarching mission to achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls.

At the same time as CSW delegates convene in New York, the U.S. government will inch closer to releasing strategies for the country-level implementation of the [Global Fragility Act (GFA)](https://www.congress.gov/bill/116th-congress/house-bill/2116) The [GFA](https://www.csis.org/analysis/policymakers-guide-global-fragility-act) led to the [U.S. Strategy to Prevent Conflict and Promote Stability](https://www.state.gov/stability-strategy/) (SPCPS), a 10-year, whole-of-government effort that portends to reshape how the United States approaches these challenges, [starting in nine countries](https://www.csis.org/analysis/global-fragility-strategy-gets-refresh): Papua New Guinea (PNG), Mozambique, Libya, Haiti, and five countries across Coastal West Africa. The GFA does not have a congressionally mandated gender focus and thus the SPCPS does not overly focus on gender; however, country strategies will hopefully acknowledge that gender inequality contributes to much of the state fragility in question. Women are left out of decisionmaking spaces, excluded from the paid labor force, deprived of access to valuable assets, and regularly experience gender-based violence to varying degrees in each of these nine countries—all dynamics that can exacerbate state fragility. These dynamics can be mitigated, at least in part, through technology.

That’s why this year’s CSW is on to something: innovation and technology can be powerful in addressing these inequalities and, as such, should be seen as critical tools within the SPCPS implementor’s toolkit. Technology—when available and accessible— could provide a means to scale the impact of SPCPS programming, potentially expanding reach to populations that might otherwise be excluded due to societal norms surrounding civic participation—including women. Appropriate utilization of technology will also be critical for policymakers to coordinate the work of the SPCPS with existing policy strategies such as the one covering [women, peace, and security (WPS)](https://www.state.gov/women-peace-and-security/) issues. Moreover, technology will be essential to develop widespread and reliable local data collection mechanisms, a critical goal for SPCPS implementation.

#### **Technology as a Driver of Gender Equality and Peace**

**Developing Technology-Driven Pathways for Women’s Economic Inclusion**

Women in fragile environments are frequently excluded from the paid workforce as well as from social and economic resources—factors that feed back into fragility as women lose agency and are thus less resilient to conflict or disaster. Only [5.8 percent](https://www.technoserve.org/blog/how-one-woman-is-challenging-gender-roles-in-mozambique/#:~:text=In%20Mozambique%2C%20strongly%20held%20beliefs,and%20care%20for%20the%20children) of women in Mozambique held paid jobs in 2018 and had, on average, only [1.4 years](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/opinion/2021/03/08/time-for-bold-action-to-advance-gender-parity-in-mozambique) of schooling and limited access to land titles. This pattern holds across other SPCPS countries, even those where women have more education. For example, while over half of Libyan women have reached secondary education or higher—on par with Libyan men—an estimated [26 percent](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1236584/unemployment-rate-in-libya-by-gender/) of women remain unemployed in 2023.

Current technology-focused projects within partner countries provide opportunities for women to remotely learn marketable skills, connect and share best practices between female tech entrepreneurs, as well as take part in the growth and development of their community’s digital infrastructure. In Libya and other fragile contexts where women may not be able to regularly or safely leave their homes to go to work, projects such as [She Codes](https://shecodes.ly/about-us/) teach women coding skills as well as help them find flexible, online work opportunities that will allow them to earn money from their homes. For female entrepreneurs already working in the tech space, the U.S. Embassy hosted PNG, the Solomon Islands, and Vanuatu for a [virtual information communication technology (](https://pg.usembassy.gov/empowering-women-in-ict-workshop/)[ICT) workshops](https://pg.usembassy.gov/empowering-women-in-ict-workshop/) to bring them together to share their experiences, discuss opportunities for growth, and network from their homes. While support for these and similar initiatives will be critical to integrating women into the workforce and ameliorating economic factors of fragility, the success of these programs will depend on women’s independent access to economic resources such as savings accounts. This is a priority that is beginning to be addressed by efforts like [Business Women Connect (BWC)](https://www.technoserve.org/blog/how-one-woman-is-challenging-gender-roles-in-mozambique/#:~:text=In%20Mozambique%2C%20strongly%20held%20beliefs,and%20care%20for%20the%20children) in Mozambique, but should be greatly expanded by developing digital financial inclusion initiatives throughout the partner countries.

**Digitizing Disaster Relief: Women and Climate Change Adaptation**

Women’s exclusion from traditional economies is accelerating due to [climate change](https://www.un.org/en/chronicle/article/womenin-shadow-climate-change) and could have an outsized effect on women in GFA partner countries, opening the opportunity for the development of technology-focused programs that center women in monitoring climate change, facilitating the climate adaptation process, and developing disaster early warning systems. All nine SPCPS partner countries are littoral, rendering them especially vulnerable to sea level rise and exposure to extreme weather events. At the same time, in countries such as PNG, women make up a critical contingent of [agricultural workers](https://www.csis.org/analysis/addressing-fragility-papua-new-guinea) whose harvests will be impacted by climate change. Since women often take up most of the unpaid labor force, they take on a disproportional responsibility for ensuring their family’s adaptation to climate change. A [Rapid Gender Analysis](https://www.careevaluations.org/wp-content/uploads/CARE-Haiti-RGA-August-14-Earthquake.pdf) in Haiti determined that women took on the excess workload following the 2021 earthquake—seeking assistance, finding resources, and looking for childcare.

In PNG, technology-driven programs such as [Sea Women Melanesia](https://news.sky.com/story/papua-new-guinea-women-harness-indigenous-knowledge-and-science-to-protect-coral-reefs-12490010) have successfully integrated women into the labor force through conservation, teaching and paying indigenous women to create and monitor protected areas for marine life using underwater cameras and GPS systems. In Mozambique, where [El Niño-Southern Oscillation](https://www.adaptation-undp.org/explore/eastern-africa/mozambique) phases largely determine the likelihood of severe droughts, flooding, and extreme weather events such as [cyclones](https://www.theguardian.com/world/2023/feb/27/weather-tracker-cyclone-freddy-brings-torrential-rain-to-parts-of-africa), the government implemented an [early warning system](https://www.thenewhumanitarian.org/analysis/2021/11/1/four-ways-Mozambique-is-adapting-to-the-climate-crisis) that combines text message alerts with in-person community outreach—conducted using bicycles and megaphones—to efficiently alert people in affected areas. While Haiti has received [funding](https://reliefweb.int/report/haiti/new-funding-and-strategic-plan-boosts-haiti-s-hydro-meteorological-service#:~:text=The%20Climate%20Risk%20and%20Early%20Warning%20Services%20Initiative,droughts%20which%20regularly%20impact%20the%20vulnerable%20Caribbean%20nation.) for an early warning system, no such system exists in Libya or in the coastal West African partner countries. Employing women in the development and implementation of this programming would allow these programs to capitalize on women’s intimate knowledge of their communities, while providing them marketable skills and employment within the information technology sector. This would at least amount to a small recognition of their outsized and primarily unpaid role in rebuilding communities after a disaster—and create pathways for further employment.

**Digital Tools to Drive Women’s Civil Society Participation**

Civic participation has rightly been at the center of [Women, Peace, and Security](https://www.state.gov/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/WPS_Strategy_10_October2019.pdf) (WPS) programming; women in fragile contexts, facing the intersecting issues of violence, economic exclusion, and the burden of climate change adaptation, have been systematically deprived of agency and inclusion in decisionmaking spaces. In PNG, only [nine women](https://www.pacwip.org/country-profiles/papua-new-guinea/) have been elected to parliament since the country became independent from Australia in 1975 and efforts to set a quota for just five out of 111 parliamentarians to be women failed in 2022. This is not due to lack of interest: [167 women](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-07-06/png-election-meet-the-women-trying-to-change-history/101206648) ran for parliamentary positions in August 2022, with only two winning [seats](https://news.pngfacts.com/2022/08/png-election-female-candidate-kessy.html). Haiti, similarly, [ranks 187 out of 190](https://aceproject.org/ero-en/misc/violence-against-women-in-elections-in-haiti-an) in terms of women’s political participation, while the Coastal West African countries are doing only slightly better with between [8.4 and 18.7 percent](https://data.unwomen.org/countries) of parliamentary seats being held by women across the five countries as of 2021.

While no single digital application is going to increase the number of women in parliament, SPCPS implementors should prioritize the creation of digital tools that can be mobilized to help women understand, operate, and develop agency in civic spaces. For instance, in Cambodia, the U.S. Agency for International Development’s (USAID) [Innovations for Social Accountability Cambodia](https://www.fhi360.org/projects/innovations-social-accountability-cambodia-isac) project has employed widely used social media platforms to empower Cambodian citizens with up-to-date information on civic resources available to them within the fields of education, healthcare, and local administration and to connect them directly with service providers, thus creating mechanisms for feedback and public service accountability.

**Technology as a Tool to Fight Impunity and Combat Gender-Based Violence**

Gender-based violence (GBV) is perhaps the most obviously gendered contributor to fragility. Women and girls make up [97 percent](https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5db70e83fc0a966cf4cc42ea/t/63ff77e9b0c13e4f6e997206/1677686766755/CBB+FINAL.pdf) of conflict-related sexual violence victims while often being excluded from obtaining the resources or institutional access needed to get assistance or remove themselves from dangerous situations. In Haiti, the United Nations’ [Spotlight Initiative](https://spotlightinitiative.org/) estimated that at least [30 percent](https://news.un.org/en/story/2022/11/1131042#:~:text=It%20is%20estimated%20that%20at,of%20sexual%20abuse%20or%20violence.) of women and girls between 15 and 30 years old had experienced GBV. In PNG, a woman is beaten approximately every [30 seconds](https://www.theguardian.com/world/commentisfree/2021/oct/01/women-in-papua-new-guinea-are-suffering-an-epidemic-of-violence-the-government-must-act). Across the GFA partner countries, impunity for GBV perpetrators is a pervasive issue; only 100 people were convicted for domestic violence relative to an estimated [1.5 million cases](https://www.unitedforequalitypng.com/) of GBV in PNG in [2020.](https://019c6425-665f-436e-a27d-cf729793144f.filesusr.com/ugd/7f0c06_7579973a602943b1bb52fadf58b00453.pdf) In Libya, where women face potential prosecution for sex outside of marriage, justice might be even more difficult.

Technology can serve as an indispensable tool to fight impunity for GBV, especially digital reporting mechanisms and utilizing technology to facilitate access to potentially life-saving health and safety resources. In PNG, a national phone counseling helpline became the central mechanism for GBV victims to seek aid during the pandemic, experiencing a [75 percent increase](https://www.looppng.com/png-news/spike-lifeline-services-105052) in demand between March 2020 and April 2021. Taking this concept a step further, in Mozambique, the creation of the [InfoViolência](https://www.un.org/africarenewal/news/freeing-women-and-girls-violence-mozambique) software—combined with [community outreach](https://www.un.org/africarenewal/news/freeing-women-and-girls-violence-mozambique) conducted over WhatsApp—has been especially crucial to tackling GBV. InfoViolência allows decisionmakers to identify trends in GBV and translate them into data-based “interventions, policies, and budgets in areas where women and girls need them most.” While the creation of helplines and digital monitoring tools are important first steps to combating GBV in fragile contexts, other programming could be modeled off existing digital tools, such as Brazil’s [Mapa do Acolhimento](https://www.mapadoacolhimento.org/), which connects survivors with nearby pro bono lawyers and volunteers trained through Mapa’s online services. A similar project applied in SPCPS partner countries could help survivors evaluate potential next steps in seeking justice.

#### **The Way Forward**

**Track progress . . .**

Across SPCPS programming, using technology to develop accurate and reliable local data collection and monitoring mechanisms will be invaluable to the adaptability of each country’s strategy to realities on the ground. Fragility is inherently gendered, necessitating the collection, analysis, and integration of gender-disaggregated data into ongoing SPCPS programming. Moreover, training and paying women to report issues, monitor progress, and collect data on SPCPS progress across their communities can serve as an opportunity to connect and collaborate with local women, building on the work of organizations that already have existing programming using technology to combat gender inequality and fragility. Of critical importance in the absence of long-term U.S. government country teams over the 10-year SPCPS implementation period, this local network can serve as a foundation from which to develop local buy-in to GFA programming. Moreover, in developing women-led data collection mechanisms at the community level, country teams can feed live, primary source information directly back into data analysis platforms being maintained in Washington, such as the Department of State Bureau of Conflict and Stabilization Operation’s [Instability Monitoring & Analysis Platform](https://www.state.gov/about-us-bureau-of-conflict-and-stabilization-operations/instability-monitoring-and-analysis-platform/), allowing for programming focused precisely on areas of need as well as further honed conflict early warning systems.

Fragility is inherently gendered, necessitating the collection, analysis, and integration of gender-disaggregated data into ongoing SPCPS programming.

**. . . while closing the digital gap.**

To mobilize digital tools to combat gendered factors of fragility across the nine SPCPS countries, implementors face the difficult task of closing sizeable digital access gaps. Internet connectivity varies across the partner countries and often differs for women; only [7 percent](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/opinion/2020/12/14/more-than-just-internet-harnessing-the-digital-economy-to-rethink-the-future-in-haiti) of women in Haiti, for instance, had access to the internet as of December 2020. In PNG, owning a cell phone is sometimes viewed as a [safety risk](https://pacificwomen.org/news/digital-transformation-the-role-of-mobile-technology-in-papua-new-guinea-how-to-ensure-that-women-benefit-too/) for women who fear being targeted for theft, online harassment, or domestic violence due to an intimate partner’s jealousy. Even in regions where internet access is widespread, facilitating access to a phone or the internet is not enough; implementors must prioritize training women how to use technology fluently, with consideration for safety, privacy, and the ever-present threat of misinformation. While digital connectivity might be nascent across most of the SPCPS countries, closing the digital gender gap can and should be given central consideration at the earliest phases of implementation. [Surtab](https://stories.usaid.gov/haitis-high-tech-revolution/#page-2), a USAID-funded project, pays a women-dominated team three times Haiti’s minimum wage to manufacture tablets which are distributed to schools, businesses, and the health sector to expand the reach of these services in rural parts of the country. Such efforts are vital to the success of the SPCPS.

Great utilization of digital tools will not solve all issues women face in fragile contexts, nor should it be expected to do so. Still, SPCPS implementors should approach gender-focused digital tools as vital components of the broader conflict prevention and stabilization toolbox—a means to scale programming, reach new communities, and amplify the female voices so often left out of the conversation.

*Abigail Edwards is a research assistant with the Project on Fragility and Mobility at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) in Washington, D.C. Alexis Day is program manager for the Smart Women, Smart Power Initiative and outreach manager for the International Security Program at CSIS. Erol Yayboke is a senior fellow with the International Security Program and director of the CSIS Project on Fragility and Mobility.*

enriched_points.expanded_content: Key programs, including corporate giving initiatives and grassroots tech access, provide scholarships, mentorships, and training, empowering women in technology and fostering a diverse workforce.

enriched_points.point_title: Impact on women's access to technology
enriched_points.expanded_content: These initiatives enhance women's access to tech education, bridging the gender gap while promoting economic growth and innovation through increased participation in the tech sector.
